trajectory_summary: The call opens with a solvable barrier (townhome eligibility), clears it quickly, then stalls because the rep never found the customer's actual motivation. The customer's real interest — curiosity about solar energy use, not bill savings — was volunteered at 03:27 and walked past. The call ends on a soft callback with no locked time, no spouse coordination, and no appointment. Energy was available to build but leaked steadily after the motivation miss.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the actual outcome is a soft callback with no appointment booked, no locked time, and no spouse included. This is a fragile non-outcome: the customer said he'd call back, but there is no scheduled sit, no bill collected, and no decision-maker alignment. Should be tagged 'callback_soft_no_appointment' rather than any booked variant.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Let me discuss it with my spouse and, give me a call on this number. Okay?
primary_coaching_focus.why: The spouse was on title (confirmed at 02:54) and named as the decision gate at 06:21 — the rep had both the protocol and the information to schedule a joint appointment. Instead, Holland accepted an open-ended callback with no locked time and no spouse coordination. Per schema.protocols.spouse_decision, this is the failure mode that produces no-shows and lost deals: the setter 'wins' the objection by accepting it rather than converting it into a scheduled sit. Manager should walk Holland through the spouse-inclusion reframe and establish a floor rule: soft callbacks without a locked time and both decision-makers confirmed are not outcomes.

key_moments.quote: No. I thought you're doing it tomorrow. Thanks. Please stay on the line. That's so sad.
key_moments.stewart_read: Pre-call audio bleed — the rep's side conversation is audible to the customer before the intro begins. 'That's so sad' is an emotionally loaded fragment that the customer hears before Holland says a word. This is a professionalism and first-impression gap: the customer's first experience of Ion is ambient personal conversation, not a controlled intro.

key_moments.quote: I do I do believe that we can work with townhomes.
key_moments.stewart_read: The hedge 'I do believe' is the wrong register for a legitimacy-building moment. The customer's entire barrier was 'someone told me solar doesn't work here' — the rep's job is to replace that secondhand doubt with confident authority. 'I do believe' mirrors the uncertainty rather than resolving it. The rep had already confirmed boundary eligibility ('it looks like you are in our boundaries' at 01:08) — this second statement should have been declarative.

key_moments.quote: And then I'm assuming you're just wanting to save on your electric bills?
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer had already said 'I just wanted to see how best one could be used' at 03:42 — a curiosity-and-efficiency frame, not a bill-pain frame. The rep's assumption collapses that into the default bill-savings pitch, which the customer immediately corrects. This is the call's pivotal miss: the customer's actual motivation (energy curiosity, tight consumption habits) was available and the rep walked past it to reach for the familiar anchor.

key_moments.quote: Well, we do have a pretty good program right now. It will start saving you immediately year one as long as you have a credit score above 670.
key_moments.stewart_read: The program pitch is delivered without any emotional anchor. The customer has just told the rep he's not motivated by bill savings and that he uses electricity conservatively. The rep delivers the standard savings-forward pitch anyway — 'saving you immediately year one' — which is misaligned with the customer's stated frame. There is no flip, no curiosity creation, and no tie to what the customer actually said he wanted.
